topic: Numeric - Float Special Values: NaN, Infinity, and total_cmp

turns.content: I'm reviewing a new numerical library for our team and I keep hearing about this weird behavior where two identical floating point values don't compare as equal. Is that actually possible in Rust's f64 type? I thought the == operator should just work like it does with integers.

Yes, that is exactly how IEEE 754 floats behave in Rust when you use the standard equality operator on f64 values representing special cases. Specifically, if either operand is NaN, then a != b will always return true even if they look identical because NaN is not equal to itself and anything else. You can verify this by checking that nan_value1 == nan_value2 evaluates to false in your code while other comparisons remain standard.

That's concerning for my sorting logic right now. I'm trying to sort a vector of sensor readings using the built-in sort method, but it keeps failing whenever one reading is NaN because the comparison fails silently. Does Rust provide any way to handle this gracefully without writing custom comparators?

The good news is that since version 1.62, Rust introduced the total_cmp trait via std::cmp::Ordering which provides a total ordering for all floats including NaN. You can now replace your manual sort_with logic by implementing a closure that uses f64::total_cmp to compare any two values safely without panicking or returning None. This allows you to use simple sort_by methods on your float slices with confidence.
